skill.name: data-visualization
skill.description: Chart and visualization generation for DBX Studio. Use when a user wants to visualize data — bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, scatter plots, etc.
skill.summary: Generate charts and visualizations from database queries using the generate_chart tool
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: All 27 static findings are false positives. The 'external_commands' flags are Markdown code fence syntax (``), not shell execution. The 'weak crypto' flags match words like 'chart' which triggered pattern matchers incorrectly. The skill is documentation-only with read-only SQL examples for the generate_chart tool. No security risks detected.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: The static scanner incorrectly flagged Markdown code fences (backticks) as external command execution. Lines 10-79 contain Markdown code blocks for SQL and JSON examples, not shell commands. These are documentation examples for the generate_chart tool configuration.

content.value_statement: Transform complex database queries into clear visual insights. This skill helps Claude create charts directly from your data using the generate_chart tool with bar, line, pie, scatter, area, and histogram visualizations.
actual_capabilities: Generate bar charts for category comparisons and top-N rankings
Create line charts for time series trends and date-based analysis
Build pie charts for part-to-whole relationships (up to 7 categories)
Produce scatter plots to show correlations between two numeric values
Design area charts for cumulative trends over time
Construct histograms to display numeric column distributions
limitations: Only works with database connections that support the generate_chart tool
Pie charts limited to 7 slices for readability
Requires read-only SQL SELECT queries (cannot modify data)
Chart aesthetics depend on the generate_chart tool implementation
Large datasets may require aggregation before visualization

use_cases.title: Sales Dashboard Charts
use_cases.description: Create visual summaries of sales performance by category, region, or time period. Generate bar charts for top products, line charts for revenue trends, and pie charts for market share distribution.

use_cases.title: Database Analytics Visualizations
use_cases.description: Transform raw database queries into charts for data exploration. Visualize user signups over time, distribution of customer ages, or correlation between marketing spend and revenue.

use_cases.title: Report Graphics Generation
use_cases.description: Automatically create charts for reports and presentations. Query database tables and generate publication-ready visualizations without manual export to separate tools.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Bar Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a bar chart showing the top 10 categories by count from the orders table
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to see which categories have the most orders

prompt_templates.title: Time Series Line Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a line chart showing daily revenue trends for the past 30 days
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to visualize trends over a time period

prompt_templates.title: Pie Chart Breakdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Make a pie chart showing the distribution of order statuses
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to see proportional breakdown of status values

prompt_templates.title: Scatter Plot Correlation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scatter plot showing the relationship between customer age and total spend
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to explore correlation between two numeric fields

output_examples.input: Show me a bar chart of monthly revenue by product category
output_examples.output: Claude generates a SQL query to aggregate revenue by category, then calls generate_chart with chart_type='bar', x_axis='category', y_axis='revenue', and a descriptive title. The chart displays categories on the x-axis and revenue amounts on the y-axis.

output_examples.input: Create a line chart of daily active users for the last week
output_examples.output: Claude writes a SQL query with DATE_TRUNC to group user counts by day, filters for the past 7 days, then generates a line chart with dates on the x-axis and user counts on the y-axis showing the trend over time.

output_examples.input: Make a pie chart of order statuses
output_examples.output: Claude queries the orders table to count records by status, then generates a pie chart with each status as a slice. If more than 7 statuses exist, Claude groups smaller ones as 'Other' to maintain readability.

best_practices: Always use descriptive chart titles that include the data context and time period when relevant
Aggregate large datasets before charting to avoid performance issues and unreadable visualizations
Match chart types to your analytical goal: bar for comparisons, line for trends, pie for proportions, scatter for correlations
Limit pie charts to 7 categories or fewer, grouping remaining items as 'Other'
Use human-readable axis labels instead of raw database column names in the chart configuration
anti_patterns: Avoid pie charts with more than 7 categories as they become difficult to read and interpret
Do not chart raw row-level data without aggregation - this creates cluttered visualizations that hide insights
Never use data modification queries (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) in visualization queries - charts require SELECT statements only
Avoid creating charts without titles or with cryptic column names as axis labels

faq.question: What chart types are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports bar charts for comparisons, line charts for trends over time, pie charts for part-to-whole relationships, scatter plots for correlations between numeric values, area charts for cumulative trends, and histograms for data distributions.

faq.question: Does this skill work with any database?
faq.answer: This skill requires the generate_chart tool which is part of DBX Studio. It works with databases connected through DBX Studio that support the tool. The SQL examples use PostgreSQL syntax but can be adapted for other databases.

faq.question: Can I modify data through this skill?
faq.answer: No. This skill only generates visualizations from data using SELECT queries. It does not perform INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or any data modification operations. All queries are read-only.

faq.question: Why are pie charts limited to 7 slices?
faq.answer: Pie charts become difficult to read with more than 7 categories. The skill follows data visualization best practices by recommending grouping smaller categories as 'Other' when you have more than 7 values to display.

faq.question: How do I customize chart appearance?
faq.answer: Chart appearance is controlled through the generate_chart tool parameters. You can set the chart title, axis labels, and chart type. More advanced styling options depend on the implementation of the generate_chart tool in your environment.

faq.question: What happens if my query returns too much data?
faq.answer: Claude will aggregate the data before generating the chart. For example, if you have millions of rows, Claude will use GROUP BY and aggregation functions like COUNT, SUM, or AVG to create meaningful chart data points rather than plotting every individual row.
